topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropping onto a desk. Dr. Albright was leaving. It was a neat, professional announcement, devoid of any warmth or acknowledgment of the years of grueling late nights I’d poured into my dissertation. A tight knot formed in my stomach, a cold, acidic feeling that spread through my chest. I stared at the screen, my knuckles white where I gripped the mouse. Every carefully constructed sentence of my argument suddenly felt brittle, exposed. I wanted to smash the keyboard, to rip the printed drafts to shreds. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my office, the floorboards creaking under my sudden, agitated movements. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

I caught myself staring at the framed photo on my bookshelf—Dr. Albright smiling, looking composed and kind. The smile felt like a deliberate mockery. When the news finally sank in, it wasn't a shock, but a deep, simmering resentment boiled up inside me. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. I didn't move for a long time, just let the silence of the room amplify the churning dissatisfaction. When I finally spoke, my voice was unnervingly level, too flat. "So, what now?" I asked the empty space across the desk, the question laced with a sarcasm so sharp it stung my own ears. I picked up my notes, not to study, but to meticulously rearrange them, stacking them into a precarious tower, a silent protest against the sudden void Dr. Albright was leaving behind.

[story 3]

The administrative announcement was posted on the main bulletin board, a stark piece of paper against the institutional beige. Seeing it made my skin crawl. I felt a profound sense of injustice wash over me, a bitter, acidic burn in my throat. I walked past the board without looking at the details, focusing instead on the rhythm of my footsteps—quick, uneven, bordering on a restless stomp. Inside, I paced my room, running a hand repeatedly through my hair, the gesture sharp and jerky. I re-read the abstract of my chapter, and the words swam before my eyes, meaningless now. A surge of intense irritation made me slam my textbook shut, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet apartment. I felt a burning need to prove, in some tangible way, that my work mattered more than the person who was departing.
